topic: A person learns their childhood bully became a therapist
raw_output:
[story 1]

The polished oak of the waiting room felt cold beneath my fingertips. I stared at the appointment card, the ink seeming to mock my usual swagger. Liam. My childhood tormentor. Now, he was a licensed clinical psychologist. The thought churned in my gut, a strange mix of disbelief and a simmering annoyance. I smoothed down my jacket, the movement sharp, deliberately slow. When the door finally opened, he looked up. His eyes, the same startling blue I remembered from playground taunts, held a practiced, gentle warmth. "Hey, Maya," he said, his voice modulated, soothing. I offered a tight, almost imperceptible nod, my jaw set hard. "Mr. Hayes," I managed, my tone clipped, refusing to let any warmth seep into the exchange. I sat down, arms crossed tightly over my chest, every muscle coiled, waiting for him to falter.

[story 2]

I walked into the office expecting confrontation, maybe a lecture, anything but this quiet, beige space. Liam was sitting behind a desk, looking impossibly composed in a crisp white shirt. He offered a small, practiced smile. "Welcome," he murmured, gesturing toward a chair. I didn't sit immediately. I paced the perimeter of the room, my boots clicking a rhythmic, impatient beat on the linoleum floor. Every glance I threw his way was a challenge, a silent declaration that whatever he thought he knew about me was entirely wrong. When he finally invited me to sit, I slid into the chair as if it were an unwelcome imposition. My shoulders remained hiked up near my ears, a physical barrier against any perceived intimacy. "So," I began, my voice rougher than intended, "what exactly are we doing here?"

[story 3]

The air in the consultation room smelled faintly of lavender and old paper. I watched Liam as he reviewed my intake forms, his brow furrowed in concentration. He was softer now, the sharp edges of the boy I knew sanded down by years of professional composure. I felt a distinct tension building behind my ribs, a need to push back against this manufactured calm. I leaned forward, resting my elbows on my knees, my gaze locked onto his. "Don't pretend this is some grand intervention," I stated, my voice low and challenging. I let a flicker of impatience cross my face, a visible tightening around my mouth. I waited, refusing to break eye contact, daring him to react to the sheer force of my refusal to play the part he expected.
